coredata.dc:title: Porous g-C3N4 and α-FeOOH bridged by carbon dots as synergetic visible-light-driven photo-fenton catalysts for contaminated water remediation
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Xiaocui
Zhao, Qingshan
Guo, Fang
Xia, Guangsen
Tan, Xiaojie
Lv, Huiyuan
Feng, Zhaoxuan
Wu, Wenting
Zheng, Jingtang
Wu, Mingbo
coredata.dc:description: Merging photocatalysis with Fenton-like reaction provides a promising synergetic advanced oxidation technology for water treatment, whereas the rational design of efficient cooperative photo-Fenton system still remains a great challenge. In this study, a ternary α-FeOOH/carbon dots/porous g-C3N4 (α-FeOOH/CDs/p-GCN) composite is developed as visible-light-driven heterogeneous photo-Fenton catalyst for contaminated water remediation. α-FeOOH nanorods are in-situ grown on porous g-C3N4 substrates, among which CDs are intercalated as high-speed electron transmission bridges, simultaneously preventing the photogenerated electron-hole recombination and accelerating the Fe3+/Fe2+ redox cycle. The distinctive heterostructure and synergetic catalytic effect endow α-FeOOH/CDs/p-GCN with superior efficiency, reusability, and universality for recalcitrant pollutant elimination, the reaction rate of which is nearly 13.6, 7.3, 3.4, 1.4, and 5.1 times higher than that of α-FeOOH, bulk g-C3N4 (b-GCN), p-GCN, α-FeOOH/p-GCN, and α-FeOOH/CDs/b-GCN, respectively. The FeOOH/CDs/p-GCN also demonstrates excellent tolerance to realistic environmental matrices including deionized water, sea water, river water, wastewater, and underground water. HO• and •O2 − radicals are proved to be the predominant contributors in the photo-Fenton system. This work provides an efficient synergetic visible-light-driven photo-Fenton catalyst possessing great potential for practical contaminated water remediation.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The band gap of the samples was estimated by related curve of (αhν)2 or or (αhν)1/2 vs. photon energy plotted, and the redox potential was measured by the Mott-Schottky method. As shown in Figure S8a, c, the band gap (Eg) value of p-GCN and α-FeOOH are tested to be 2.420 and 2.210 eV, respectively, which inosculates with the excellent visible light response property. The Mott-Schottky plots of p-GCN and α-FeOOH both exhibit a positive linear slope and intercept of −1.488 and −0.015 V (vs. Ag/AgCl), respectively (Figure S8b, d). Accordingly, the flat band potential (Efb) are determined to be −0.912 and 0.561 V (vs. NHE), respectively (detailed calculative process in the supporting information). As a n-type semiconductor, the conduction band (CB) potential of α-FeOOH equals to Efb (ECB = 0.561 V). Since the CB potential of p-GCN is generally 0.2 V higher than Efb [50,51], the ECB is determined to be −0.712 V. According to the equation: ECB = EVB − Eg, the valence band (EVB) potentials of p-GCN and α-FeOOH are calculated to be 1.708 V and 2.771 V, respectively. As shown in Figure S8e, the Mott-Schottky plot of CDs shows an intercept of 0.123 V (vs. Ag/AgCl), and the Efb of CDs is calculated to be 0.698 V (vs. NHE), which equals to the Fermi level of CDs. Considering the much lower Fermi level of CDs than the standard reduction potential of Fe3+/Fe2+ (E0 (Fe3+/Fe2+) = 0.77 V vs. NHE), the photogenerated electrons shows great potential to transfer from p-GCN to α-FeOOH and participate in the Fe3+/Fe2+ Fenton cycle [52].This work was financially supported by the National Key R&D Program of China (NO. 2019YFA0708700), China; the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (No. ZR2019QB016, ZR2018ZC1458), China; the Fundamental Research Fund for the Central Universities (No. 18 C × 02015 A), China; the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. U1662113), China; the Financial Support from Taishan Scholar Project of Shandong Province of China (ts201712020), China; Technological Leading Scholar of 10000 Talent Project (No. W03020508), China.

This work was financially supported by the National Key R&D Program of China (NO. 2019YFA0708700 ), China; the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (No. ZR2019QB016 , ZR2018ZC1458 ), China; the Fundamental Research Fund for the Central Universities (No. 18 C × 02015 A ), China; the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. U1662113 ), China; the Financial Support from Taishan Scholar Project of Shandong Province of China ( ts201712020 ), China; Technological Leading Scholar of 10000 Talent Project (No. W03020508 ), China.

(A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Porous g-C3N4 and α-FeOOH bridged by carbon dots as synergetic visible-light-driven photo-fenton catalysts for contaminated water remediation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemical Engineering, College of New Energy, Institute of New Energy, State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing, China University of Petroleum (East China), Qingdao, 266580, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemical Engineering, College of New Energy, Institute of New Energy, State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing, China University of Petroleum (East China), Qingdao, 266580, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Merging photocatalysis with Fenton-like reaction provides a promising synergetic advanced oxidation technology for water treatment, whereas the rational design of efficient cooperative photo-Fenton system still remains a great challenge. In this study, a ternary α-FeOOH/carbon dots/porous g-C3N4 (α-FeOOH/CDs/p-GCN) composite is developed as visible-light-driven heterogeneous photo-Fenton catalyst for contaminated water remediation. α-FeOOH nanorods are in-situ grown on porous g-C3N4 substrates, among which CDs are intercalated as high-speed electron transmission bridges, simultaneously preventing the photogenerated electron-hole recombination and accelerating the Fe3+/Fe2+ redox cycle. The distinctive heterostructure and synergetic catalytic effect endow α-FeOOH/CDs/p-GCN with superior efficiency, reusability, and universality for recalcitrant pollutant elimination, the reaction rate of which is nearly 13.6, 7.3, 3.4, 1.4, and 5.1 times higher than that of α-FeOOH, bulk g-C3N4 (b-GCN), p-GCN, α-FeOOH/p-GCN, and α-FeOOH/CDs/b-GCN, respectively. The FeOOH/CDs/p-GCN also demonstrates excellent tolerance to realistic environmental matrices including deionized water, sea water, river water, wastewater, and underground water. HO• and •O2 − radicals are proved to be the predominant contributors in the photo-Fenton system. This work provides an efficient synergetic visible-light-driven photo-Fenton catalyst possessing great potential for practical contaminated water remediation.

ce:section.ce:para: Water pollution has become a major threat to human health, of which the substantial rise has been considered as one of the biggest challenges in environmental problems. Among the various developed methods to deal with water and wastewater treatment, advanced oxidation processes (AOPs), by producing strongly oxidizing species such as hydroxyl radicals (HO•), take advantages of ideally destroying recalcitrant and toxic pollutants under ambient conditions [1,2]. Developing efficient and low consumptive AOPs technologies to remove pollutants from contaminated water is of great significance for a sustainable society.

As a typical AOP, Fenton technology is widely applied in water treatment processes. However, traditional homogeneous Fenton system suffers from problems such as large amounts of iron-containing sludge and narrow optimal pH range, which inspires the exploration of heterogeneous Fenton-like catalysts [3,4]. Owing to the abundance, low cost and peroxidase-like activity, iron-based materials such as Fe3O4, Fe2O3, and FeOOH show great potential as promising Fenton-like catalysts [5,6]. Nevertheless, most of these catalysts exhibit inferior activities and recyclability due to the low Fe3+/Fe2+ cycle efficiency on the catalyst surface [7,8], which seriously hinders their practical applications. Therefore, it is still urgent to develop high-performance heterogeneous Fenton-like system for contaminated water remediation. In recent years, semiconductor photocatalysis has also attracted intensive attention as an emerging photocatalytic AOP for pollutant elimination [9,10]. When photoexcited by lamp or solar radiation, pairs of conduction band electron and valence band hole are created, which subsequently induces the generation of superoxide radical (•O2 −) and HO• to degrade various pollutants in the contaminated water [11]. Considering their respective characteristics, merging photocatalysis with Fenton-like reaction to build a heterogeneous photo-Fenton system provides a promising synergetic advanced oxidation technology.

With an appropriate band gap of 2.70 eV, graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) has been rendered as an intriguing visible light responsive metal-free semiconductor photocatalyst for water remediation [12]. However, typical bulk g-C3N4 possesses a poor surface area and undergoes severe electron-hole recombination, which seriously restricts the catalytic performance [13]. Thus, it is urgent to break through the conventional concept and develop novel g–C3N4–based system with high efficiency for water treatment. Methods towards regulating the active sites to improve the photocatalytic activity of g–C3N4–based materials, including morphology regulation, surface treatment, heteroatom doping, and interfacial interaction, have been also expounded [14]. For instance, Mao et al. tunes Fe-doped g-C3N4 bulk structure into multi-layer structure with uniformly dispersed mesopores to expose more active sites, which presents 7.5 times higher catalytic activities in the Fenton-like process [13]. Yang et al. modulates g-C3N4 by molecular engineering of supramolecular precursor to realize superior hydrogen evolution rate, which exhibits about 30.1 times higher activity than that of the bulk C3N4 under visible light irradiation [15]. Even so, the efficient combination of photocatalysis process and Fenton-like system still remains a big challenge. Coupling of iron species with g-C3N4 brings new opportunity for the construction of efficient visible-light-driven heterogeneous photo-Fenton catalysts. For instance, Zhao et al. anchored FeOOH quantum dots on g-C3N4 as a photo-Fenton catalyst for the degradation of phenol and methylene orange [3]. The photogenerated electron produced by visible light exciting g-C3N4 can participate in the cycling of Fe3+/Fe2+ and improve the catalytic efficiency. Accordingly, rational design of the catalyst heterostructure is significant to combine photocatalysis with Fenton-like catalysis together. Especially, the efficient transmission of photogenerated electrons between solid interfaces are the crucial factors to construct a high-performance cooperative photo-Fenton system.

Carbon dots (CDs) are typically nanoparticles with a diameter of less than 10 nm, which are composed of sp2 carbon fused by sp3 hybridized carbon insertions [16]. On account of the capability of harnessing long wavelength light and energy exchange, CDs can be utilized as a powerful energy-transfer component in photocatalyst for application in environmental issues. For instance, Wang et al. synthesized a g-C3N4/S-CDs composite that displays a 4.97 times faster reaction rate for TC photodegradation than pristine g-C3N4 owing to the enhanced production and separation of electron-hole pairs induced by CDs [17]. Tian et al. developed CDs/Cu modification mesoporous iron oxide that exhibited a high efficiency towards ofloxacin degradation over a wide pH range (pH = 3.6–10.0) on account of the excellent electron transformation/energy exchange property of CDs [18]. The zero-dimensional morphology and excellent electron transfer ability render CDs as a promising linkage between the photo and Fenton-like catalyst interfaces.

Herein, by employing CDs as high-speed electron transmission bridges, a ternary α-FeOOH/carbon dots/porous g-C3N4 (α-FeOOH/CDs/p-GCN) composite was rationally designed and fabricated as a visible-light-driven heterogenous photo-Fenton catalyst for contaminated water remediation. α-FeOOH nanorods were in-situ grown and tightly anchored on porous g-C3N4 substrates interfacial connected by the zero-dimensional CDs. The catalytic efficiency of α-FeOOH/CDs/p-GCN towards a series of recalcitrant pollutants were evaluated under visible light irradiation. Owing to the unique nanostructure and synergetic catalytic effect, the α-FeOOH/CDs/p-GCN composite exhibited superior catalytic efficiency than α-FeOOH, p-GCN, α-FeOOH/p-GCN, bulk g-C3N4 (b-GCN), and α-FeOOH/CDs/b-GCN, which simultaneously showed excellent stability and universality. The catalytic performance of α-FeOOH/CDs/p-GCN was also systematically evaluated in various realistic water matrices to validate the practical application potential. The role of CDs was intensively studied, and the possible mechanism for the photo-Fenton reaction process on the surface of α-FeOOH/CDs/p-GCN was proposed.

Ferrous sulfate (FeSO4·7H2O), urea (CH4N2O), sodium citrate (Na3C6H5O7·2H2O), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), ethylene glycol (EG), Rhodamine B (RhB), disodium ethylenediamine acetate (EDTA-2Na), ascorbic acid (AA) and tert butyl alcohol (TBA), calcium chloride (CaCl2), potassium chloride (KCl), magnesium chloride hexahydrate (MgCl2·6H2O), sodium chloride (NaCl), sodium nitrate (NaNO3), trisodium phosphate (Na3PO4), sodium carbonate (NaCO3), and sodium sulfate (Na2SO4) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Melamine, 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrrolidine-N-oxide (DMPO), methyl orange (MO), methylene blue (MB), tetracycline (TC), p-Nitrophenol (p-NP), acid fuchsin (AF), and humic acid (HA) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Nafion solution (5 wt%) was obtained from Shanghai Lingfeng Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. The simulated wastewater solutions were typically prepared with ultrapure water. To validate the practical application potential, realistic water matrices including wastewater, underground water, seawater, deionized water, and river water were also employed as the substances.

p-GCN was prepared according to a reported method [19]. Specifically, a certain amount of urea was grounded in an agate mortar and tiled into a quartz boat. Then, the powder was calcined at 550 °C for 4 h with a heating rate of 2 °C min−1 in nitrogen atmosphere. After cooling naturally, the attained yellow product was grounded into powder and dispersed into deionized water accompanied by ultrasound for 2 h. Finally, p-GCN was obtained through filtration and vacuum drying at 60 °C for 10 h. As a control, bulk g-C3N4 (b-GCN) was prepared through the same method by using melamine as a substitute precursor.

CDs were synthesized by a simple modified process according to previous literatures [20–22]. Typically, 2.0 g sodium citrate was grounded in an agate mortar, tiled into the quartz boat, and calcined at 350 °C for 3 h with a heating rate of 5 °C min−1 in nitrogen atmosphere. After cooled to room temperature, the prepared CDs was dispersed in water and dialyzed for 2 days to remove the residual small molecules.

The α-FeOOH/CDs/p-GCN was synthesized through a facile one-pot deposition method as shown in Scheme 1 . 2.5 mL CDs solution (0.4 mg mL−1) was dispersed into 20 mL p-GCN suspension (2.5 mg mL−1). After treated by ultrasonic for 30 min, 500 μL EG was added into the mixture under intense agitation. Subsequently, 20 mL FeSO4∙7H2O solution (25 mg mL−1) was dropwise added into the mixture, which was heated with refluxing at 100 °C for 1 h. α-FeOOH nanorods were grown on the p-GCN substrate interfacial bridged CDs. The product was washed with deionized water and collected after vacuum drying at 60 °C for 10 h, noted α-FeOOH/2%CDs/p-GCN. To investigate the influence of CDs loading, α-FeOOH/x% CDs/p-GCN (x means the weight percentage of CDs toward p-GCN, x = 0%, 2%, 5%) composites were prepared. As controls, α-FeOOH/p-GCN, CDs/p-GCN, and CDs/α-FeOOH were synthesized with a similar method except for the addition of CDs, FeSO4∙7H2O, and p-GCN, respectively. α-FeOOH was prepared without the addition of p-GCN and CDs [23]. α-FeOOH/CDs/b-GCN was prepared by replacing p-GCN with b-GCN.

The morphologies and structure of the samples were investigated by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, S-4800, Hitachi) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Tecnai G2 F30, FEI). The phase identifying of the catalysts were investigated by X-ray diffraction (XRD) using a PANalytical X-ray diffractometer with Cu-Kα radiation (k = 0.15406 nm). The elemental composition and chemical state were obtained by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) using a Thermol scientific Escalab 250Xi spectrometer with Al-Kα radiation. The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were measured on a pressed KBr pellet by employing a Nicolet 6700 spectrometer. Raman spectroscopy was performed using a Raman spectrometer (Raman, Renishaw Invia Microconfocal System, England) in the range of 200–3000 cm−1 at a 532 nm wavelength. UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (UV–vis DRS) were obtained for the dry-pressed disk samples using a UV–vis spectrometer (UV-2450, Shimadzu) with BaSO4 as a reflectance standard. Fluorescence spectra were measured by a spectrofluorometer (F-97 Pro, Technology). Electron spin resonance (ESR) results were revealed on a JES-X320 by employing DMPO as a spin-trap reagent.

Photoelectrochemical measurements were conducted on CHI 1140c workstation with a standard three-electrode system [24]. Typically, 2.0 mg sample was ultrasonically dispersed in 50 μL ethanol with 10 μL Nafion solution (5 wt%) and 50 μL ultrapure water to form a uniform slurry. FTO electrode deposited with the slurry, platinum sheet, and saturated Ag/AgCl were employed as the working electrode, counter electrode, and reference electrode, respectively. For the photocurrent versus time analysis and cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurements, a 300 W Xe lamp (cutting off λ < 420 nm) and Na2SO4 (0.5 M) were employed as the light source and electrolyte. Cyclic voltammograms were measured at scan rate of 100 mV s−1 at ambient temperature. Mott-Schottky tests were carried out with the potentials ranged from −5 V to −0.6 V (vs Ag/AgCl) at the frequency of 1.0 kHz. Electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) measurements were measured at open-circuit potential with frequency range of 0.01–1000 kHz on CHI 760e workstation with a standard three-electrode system.

The catalytic performance of the catalysts was generally evaluated by employing RhB MO, MB, TC, p-NP, and AF as simulated pollutants under visible light irradiation. Specifically, 10 mg catalyst was firstly dispersed in certain pollutant solution (50 mL, 10 mg L−1). Prior to visible light illumination, the suspension was stirred in the dark to reach adsorption-desorption equilibrium. Then 130 μL H2O2 (30%) was added into the suspension in a water-cooled reactor under visible light irradiation by using a 300 W Xe lamp with a 420 nm cut-off filter. Three milliliter aliquots were collected at certain time intervals and centrifuged to remove the solid. The concentration was evaluated by recording the maximum absorbance with a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Gold Spectrumlab 54, Shanghai Lengguang).

To elucidate the reactive species produced in the photo-Fenton system, EDTA-2Na, AA, and TBA were employed as scavengers to trap photogenerated hole (h+), •O2 −, and HO•, respectively [25–27]. Except for the addition of scavenger, the experimental procedure was the same with the above photo-Fenton catalytic process.

The phase structures of the samples were firstly analyzed by XRD patterns. As showed in Fig. 1 a, the multi diffraction peaks of α-FeOOH are in accordance with orthorhombic phase goethite (JCPDS No. 99–0055). Two broad peaks at 13.3° and 27.7° can be observed for p-GCN, which are well indexed as (100) and (002) diffraction planes associated with the in-planar structural packing motif and interplanar graphitic stacking of conjugated aromatic systems, respectively [28]. When combined together, XRD patterns of α-FeOOH/p-GCN and α-FeOOH/CDs/p-GCN exhibit all the peaks of p-GCN and α-FeOOH, suggesting the successful deposition of α-FeOOH on p-GCN. No additional diffraction peaks can be detected in α-FeOOH/CDs/p-GCN, which should be ascribed to the ultrafine particle size and low content of CDs. To illustrate the influence of CDs on the composite structure, α-FeOOH/CDs/p-GCN with different CDs contents were prepared and characterized (Figure S1a). The sharp and narrow peaks of α-FeOOH gradually become wider and smaller with the increasing addition of CDs, implying the formation of smaller sized α-FeOOH. Nevertheless, excessive CDs would impede the growth of α-FeOOH and result in weak signals of α-FeOOH for α-FeOOH/5%CDs/p-GCN.

Fig. 1b presents the FTIR spectra of the samples. The α-FeOOH spectrum shows peaks at around 887 and 790 cm−1 corresponding to the bending vibration of Fe–O–H, while the bands located at 622–660 cm−1 is assigned to the stretching vibration of Fe–O [29]. The broad band at 2925–3433 cm−1 should be attributed to the stretching modes of O–H from α-FeOOH and adsorbed water [30]. For the pristine p-GCN, the broad absorption peaks at 3000–3600 cm−1 mainly result from the stretching vibration of O–H and N–H. The multi peaks at 1642, 1570, 1458, 1417, 1318, and 1247 cm−1 are ascribed to the stretching vibrations of CN conjugated heterocyclic structures (C–N and CN), and the 810 cm−1 peak arises from the s-triazine ring vibrations of g-C3N4 [31]. The spectra of α-FeOOH/p-GCN and α-FeOOH/CDs/p-GCN present all the characteristic vibrations from α-FeOOH and p-GCN. Moreover, the absorption peaks at 3408, 1581, 1401, 1278, and 1108 cm−1 indicate that CDs are rich in hydrophilic functional groups such as amino, hydroxyl, and carboxyl group, while the peaks at 1030, 2924, and 2850 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of CC, CN, and methyl methylene, as shown in Figure. S1c [32]. Due to the overlapping with strong adsorption of g-C3N4, typical CDs bands cannot be observed in the spectra of α-FeOOH/CDs/p-GCN with different CDs loadings (Figure S1b). As evidenced by the spectrum of α-FeOOH/CDs without p-GCN, the peaks at 1650–1740, 1550, and 1039 cm−1 correspond to the vibrations of C–O–C, CO, and CC bonds, respectively, indicating the intercalation of CDs. Raman spectra of CDs, α-FeOOH/p-GCN, and α-FeOOH/CDs/p-GCN samples in the range 200–3000 cm−1 are shown in Figure S1d. Two scattering peaks at 1359 and 1599 cm−1 shown in the spectrum of CDs are ascribed to the disordered D band resulting from a break in the symmetry at the edges or defects and ordered G band related to the C–C stretching mode, respectively [33]. Additionally, two weak peaks at 922 and 1043 cm−1 are attributed the basic stretching vibration and bending vibration of CO, respectively. The characteristic peaks of g-C3N4 at 551, 787 and 1086 cm−1, as well as the characteristic peak of α-FeOOH at 471 cm−1, can be observed in the spectrum of α-FeOOH/p-GCN [34,35]. The characteristic peaks of CDs, g-C3N4, and α-FeOOH can be all observed in the α-FeOOH/CDs/p-GCN spectra. Remarkably, a blue shift of the D band and G band, as well as the α-FeOOH peak shifting to 461 cm−1, suggest an strong interaction between CDs and α-FeOOH/p-GCN [36].

XPS analysis was performed to further confirm the surface composition and chemical state of α-FeOOH/CDs/p-GCN. The XPS survey spectrum indicates the existence of C, N, O, and Fe elements in the composite (Fig. 2 a). The C 1s XPS shows multi peaks centered at 284.6, 285.9, 288.4, and 288.8 eV, which are indexed to C–C (sp2), C–O/CN, C–N/CO, and C–NH2, respectively (Fig. 2b). As shown in Fig. 2c, the N 1s XPS spectrum can be deconvoluted into four peaks, of which the peaks at 398.9, 401.0, and 404.1 eV are attributed to sp2 hybridized aromatic N in triazine rings (C–NC), C–N–H, and terminal –NH2 groups, respectively [27,37]. The remaining peak at 399.7 eV relates to N-(C)3 groups linking structural motif (C6N7) or (C)2–N–H connecting with structural defects and incomplete condensation of g-C3N4 [38]. As to the O 1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 2d), the peaks at 529.6 and 531.1 eV are assigned to Fe–O and Fe–O–H originated from α-FeOOH, respectively [3,39]. Whereas the C–OH/CO peak at 532.5 eV is associated with the surface oxygen-containing groups of CDs [40]. Notably, the Fe–O–C peak at 530.5 eV suggests an intimate linkage of α-FeOOH with the CDs/p-GCN matrix. Study on the Fe 2p XPS spectrum reveals two broad peaks centered at 711.3 and 724.9 eV, corresponding to Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2, respectively (Fig. 2e). The satellite peaks at 719.8 and 733.9 eV further illuminate the presence of the Fe3+ ions in α-FeOOH [41]. These results indicate successful construction of the ternary α-FeOOH/CDs/p-GCN composite.

SEM and TEM measurements were further conducted to compare the morphologies and microstructures of bulk g-C3N4, p-GCN, and α-FeOOH/CDs/p-GCN. Compared with typical bulk g-C3N4 (b-GCN, shown in Figure S2), SEM image of p-GCN displays a porous configuration with interconnected nanowalls (Fig. 3 a), which is beneficial to the mass diffusion and visible light response [42]. The porous and lamellar structure of p-GCN is further confirmed by the TEM image, as shown in Fig. 3c. SEM and TEM images of α-FeOOH/CDs/p-GCN show the well composition of clubbed α-FeOOH with p-GCN (Fig. 3b, d). Magnified TEM image reveals α-FeOOH nanorods with a diameter of ∼10 nm and length of ∼52 nm are uniformly dispersed on the surface of the p-GCN substrate (Fig. 3e). The appearance of ultrasmall spherical dots (marked with red cycles), which are identical with the morphology of CDs with a diameter of ∼1.97 ± 0.014 nm (Figure S3), reveals the homogeneous distribution of CDs at the solid interfaces of p-GCN and α-FeOOH [43,44]. As shown in Fig. 3f, high-resolution TEM image of α-FeOOH/CDs/p-GCN further provides insights of nanocrystals with lattice spacing of 0.22 nm and 0.34 nm, agreeing well with the (100) lattice plane of CDs and the (120) lattice plane of α-FeOOH, respectively. Thus, α-FeOOH nanorods are intimately and homogeneously anchored on porous g-C3N4 substrates interfacial connected by the ultrafine zero-dimensional CDs.

UV–Vis DRS were conducted to investigate the optical properties of the samples. As can be seen in Fig. 4 a, the light absorption edges of p-GCN and α-FeOOH are located at about 455 and 750 nm, respectively, implying a wider visible light absorption region of α-FeOOH [45]. The α-FeOOH/p-GCN composite exhibits the hybrid absorption properties of α-FeOOH and p-GCN. Remarkably, introduction of CDs can further promote the visible light absorption ability and broaden the absorption region for α-FeOOH/CDs/p-GCN. Further study on the influence of CDs amount reveals a slightly wider absorption region with the increasing of CDs (Figure S4). The separation and recombination efficiency of electron-hole pairs was further investigated via PL analysis (Fig. 4b). Owing to the versatile porous structure, p-GCN shows a much weaker emission peak at 475 nm than that of bulk g-C3N4 (centered at 444 nm), suggesting a lower recombination of electrons and holes (Figure S5a). While α-FeOOH exhibits a small and weak PL signal, which should be ascribed to its low response to light. Interestingly, α-FeOOH/CDs/p-GCN emerges the weakest signal compared with p-GCN and α-FeOOH/p-GCN. Considering the crucial role of CDs, PL spectra of α-FeOOH/x% CDs/p-GCN composites were conducted and reveal the gradually weak emission signals along with the increasing CDs amount (Figure S5b), indicating CDs can efficiently promote electron transfer and inhibit electron-hole recombination [46]. These prominent optical properties of α-FeOOH/CDs/p-GCN are conductive to the light harvesting and electron transmission, which render it as a promising visible-light-driven catalyst candidate for photo-Fenton reactions.

The Photo-Fenton performance of the synthesized nanomaterials was evaluated by the elimination of various recalcitrant pollutants. RhB was firstly employed as a model pollutant, and the degradation rate of RhB under different reaction conditions is shown in Fig. 5 a. Without α-FeOOH/CDs/p-GCN, the concentration of RhB shows hardly any change in the H2O2 and visible light system, suggesting the necessity for a catalyst. α-FeOOH/CDs/p-GCN only results in a slight decrease of RhB in the absence of H2O2 and visible light, which should be ascribed to physical absorption. The Fenton-like process was performed in the presence of H2O2 in the dark and shows a removal efficiency of 18% with prolonged time to 120 min, reflecting an inferior Fenton-like activity (Figure S6a). This should be due to the low conversion rate of Fe3+ to Fe2+ reduced by H2O2. Moreover, the α-FeOOH/CDs/p-GCN also provides as a photocatalytic catalyst and affords a degradation rate of 50.8% under visible light irradiation. Remarkably, the removal efficiency is significantly increased to 97.1% within 20 min in the presence of H2O2 and visible light, demonstrating that α-FeOOH/CDs/p-GCN can merge Fenton-like reaction with photocatalysis process to build a synergetic photo-Fenton system.

Fig. 5b provides a comprehensive comparison of the photo-Fenton catalytic performance of different catalysts in H2O2+visible light system. Compared to pure p-GCN and α-FeOOH, the combination with CDs to construct p-GCN/CDs and α-FeOOH/CDs can improve the catalytic efficiency, revealing the positive effect of CDs. On account of the synergetic effect, α-FeOOH/p-GCN shows enhanced removel rate of RhB. In particular, once CDs is introduced to the composite, the α-FeOOH/CDs/p-GCN exhibits superior photo-Fenton catalytic performance, suggesting promoted electron transfer efficiency between p-GCN and α-FeOOH induced by CDs. As seen in Figure S6b, degradation over the above catalysts is found to follow a pseudo-first-order kinetics. Accordingly, the average rate constants are listed in Fig. 5c. Apparently, α-FeOOH/CDs/p-GCN delivers the highest rate constant of 0.1699 min−1, which is nearly 13.6, 7.3, 3.4, 1.4, and 5.1 times higher than that of α-FeOOH, b-GCN, p-GCN, α-FeOOH/p-GCN, and α-FeOOH/CDs/b-GCN, respectively. To validate the employment of p-GCN as the matrix, bulk g-C3N4 (b-GCN) was chosen as a reference control. As shown in Fig. 5d, the p-GCN exhibits much better catalytic behavior than b-GCN, which is identical to the trend of α-FeOOH/CDs/p-GCN compared with α-FeOOH/CDs/b-GCN. The removal of RhB can also be expressed by a pseudo-first order kinetics, and the order of the average rate constants follows: α-FeOOH/CDs/p-GCN > α-FeOOH/CDs/b-GCN > p-GCN > b-GCN (0.1699 min−1 > 0.0334 min−1 > 0.0414 min−1 > 0.0232 min−1), as shown in Figure S6c-d. Hence, the porous structure of g-C3N4 plays an important role to fabricate the effective photo-Fenton catalyst, which can provide larger surface area for light harvesting due to multiple-scattering effects, leading to enhanced generation of electron-hole pairs [38,47]. Considering the crucial role of CDs, the catalytic efficiency over α-FeOOH/CDs/p-GCN composites with different CDs contents were also evaluated. As descripted in Fig. 5e, all the samples exhibit excellent catalytic performance, and α-FeOOH/2%CD/p-GCN stands out among them. Therefore, appropriate amount of CDs can efficiently promote the photo-Fenton reaction, whereas excess CDs would affect the formation of α-FeOOH and shield light adsorption, resulting in a depressed catalytic activity. Fig. 5f presents the reusability and stability performance of α-FeOOH/CDs/p-GCN. With consecutive six catalytic cycles, the catalyst maintains its high catalytic efficiency and reaction rate without significant deterioration. After each run, the reaction solution was measured, and trace of iron species could be detected, unraveling the robust stability of α-FeOOH/CDs/p-GCN, as well as the smooth proceeding of Fenton-like process on the solid catalyst surface.

In order to evaluate the general applicability of α-FeOOH/CDs/p-GCN, the elimination of various pollutants including methylene blue (MB), methyl orange (MO), tetracycline (TC), acid fuchsin (AF), and p-nitrophenol (p-NP) were measured, as shown in Fig. 6 a. The catalyst exhibits 9.6% and 6.7% adsorption removal of MB and TC, respectively, whereas it exhibits low adsorption efficiency towards MO, AF, and p-NP. It is a remarkable fact that all of the pollutants expect for p-NP display a satisfactory removal efficiency of over 90% within 90 min. As seen in Figure S6e, the degradation processes follow pseudo-first-order kinetics, and corresponding average rate constants of MB, MO, TC, AF, and p-NP are 0.0442, 0.0426, 0.0269, 0.0535, and 0.0107 min−1, respectively, as listed in the right part of Fig. 6a. The results reveal excellent universality of α-FeOOH/CDs/p-GCN towards recalcitrant pollutant elimination.

More importantly, to determine the catalyst's performance in realistic environmental conditions, the degradation of RhB in water matrices including deionized water, sea water, river water, wastewater, and underground water were systematically evaluated. As shown in Fig. 6b, the removal efficiencies are 97.1%, 94.3%, 73.7%, 91.9%, and 91.4% within 20 min, respectively, with corresponding average rate constants listed in Fig. 6c. Apparently, compared with the satisfactory performance in other water matrices, the catalyst shows a relatively lower removal efficiency in river water. To investigate this phenomenon, as one of the most typical natural organic matters (NOMs), HA (humic acid) is chosen to assess the influence of natural macromolecules. The removal efficiency decreases from 97.1 to 76.7% when the HA concentration increases from 0 to 20 mg L−1 (Fig. 6d), implying the slightly negative role of HA [48]. Fig. 6e shows the effect of the initial pH on the photo-Fenton performance of α-FeOOH/CDs/p-GCN. The catalyst affords excellent performance for pollutant degradation in a wide pH range of 3–11, and specifically when the pH = 3.0 the catalyst has maximum efficiency agreeing with previous reports [4,6]. This demonstrated that photo-Fenton works over a wider pH range as compared to the traditional Fenton reaction. Moreover, as common background electrolytes in water, Ca2+, K+, Mg2+, Cl−, CO3 2−, NO3 −, PO4 3−, and SO4 2− with various concentrations are conducted to evaluate the effect of inorganic ions. Most inorganic ions show negligible influence with removal efficiencies over 97%, as can be seen Figure S7. It is a notable fact that the increasing concentration of NO3 − and CO3 2− partly suppress the degradation process, which are attributed to its capacity to scavenge HO• [49]. According to the quality parameters of the water matrices shown in Table S1, it can be inferred the relatively lower catalytic performance can be attributed to the higher concentrations of NOMs, NO3 −, and PO4 3− in river water. Still and all, the results illustrate the excellent generality and tolerance of α-FeOOH/CDs/p-GCN in realistic water matrices, demonstrating great potential for practical contaminated water remediation.

Additionally, for comparison to the state-of-the-art photocatalysts, statistics of related studies for photo-Fenton degradation are listed in Table S2 after surveying a mass of researches. Compared with the recent reported works, the α-FeOOH/CDs/p-GCN composite displays remarkably preferable contaminant elimination with less time and less catalyst dosage. Especially, as we know, the dosage of H2O2 is one of the most important parameters to evaluate the potential application for cost control. The removal efficiency and H2O2 dosage are compared and shown in Fig. 7 . By contrast, α-FeOOH/CDs/p-GCN exhibits excellent degradation efficiency of RhB, MB, MO, and TC with a quite low H2O2 dosage, further demonstrates the outstanding catalytic performance of α-FeOOH/CDs/p-GCN for contaminated water remediation.

The band gap of the samples was estimated by related curve of (αhν)2 or or (αhν)1/2 vs. photon energy plotted, and the redox potential was measured by the Mott-Schottky method. As shown in Figure S8a, c, the band gap (Eg) value of p-GCN and α-FeOOH are tested to be 2.420 and 2.210 eV, respectively, which inosculates with the excellent visible light response property. The Mott-Schottky plots of p-GCN and α-FeOOH both exhibit a positive linear slope and intercept of −1.488 and −0.015 V (vs. Ag/AgCl), respectively (Figure S8b, d). Accordingly, the flat band potential (Efb) are determined to be −0.912 and 0.561 V (vs. NHE), respectively (detailed calculative process in the supporting information). As a n-type semiconductor, the conduction band (CB) potential of α-FeOOH equals to Efb (ECB = 0.561 V). Since the CB potential of p-GCN is generally 0.2 V higher than Efb [50,51], the ECB is determined to be −0.712 V. According to the equation: ECB = EVB − Eg, the valence band (EVB) potentials of p-GCN and α-FeOOH are calculated to be 1.708 V and 2.771 V, respectively. As shown in Figure S8e, the Mott-Schottky plot of CDs shows an intercept of 0.123 V (vs. Ag/AgCl), and the Efb of CDs is calculated to be 0.698 V (vs. NHE), which equals to the Fermi level of CDs. Considering the much lower Fermi level of CDs than the standard reduction potential of Fe3+/Fe2+ (E0 (Fe3+/Fe2+) = 0.77 V vs. NHE), the photogenerated electrons shows great potential to transfer from p-GCN to α-FeOOH and participate in the Fe3+/Fe2+ Fenton cycle [52].

To shed light on the photo-Fenton reaction mechanism, transient photocurrent responses (TPR) photoelectrochemical measurements of b-GCN, p-GCN, α-FeOOH/p-GCN, and α-FeOOH/CDs/p-GCN were performed under intermittent irradiation of visible light for several on-off cycles (Fig. 8 a). The samples show obvious photocurrents when the light is switched on, while the photocurrents drop to a low level once cut off. The higher photocurrent density of p-GCN compared with b-GCN demonstrates the porous structure of g-C3N4 is conducive to the generation and separation of photogenerated electrons. However, when combined with α-FeOOH, α-FeOOH/p-GCN shows an obvious weakened photocurrent density, which should be ascribed to the shading effect of α-FeOOH and high transfer resistance between solid interfaces. Remarkably, α-FeOOH/CDs/p-GCN shows the largest photocurrent density among the samples, which should be attributed to the superior electron transmission between solid interfaces to suppress electron-hole recombination induced by CDs. EIS tests of α-FeOOH/p-GCN and α-FeOOH/CDs/p-GCN were further conducted to illustrate this result (Fig. 8b). In comparison, the smaller semicircle of α-FeOOH/CDs/p-GCN than that of α-FeOOH/p-GCN at the high-medium frequency region suggests much smaller charge-transfer resistance, while the higher slope at the low frequency region indicates the higher ion diffusion kinetics across the sample, providing direct evidence for the promoted electron transfer ability arising from the introduction of CDs [22,53]. CV curve of α-FeOOH/CDs/p-GCN was tested to illuminate the Fenton-like process (Fig. 8c). None oxidation peak can be observed at 0.4–1.2 V in the dark, suggesting the absence of Fe2+ species in the sample. Interestingly, after treated with visible light, the α-FeOOH/CDs/p-GCN electrode shows a slightly lower reductive current and obvious enhanced oxidative current, which is attributed to the reduction of Fe3+ and appearance of Fe2+ after visible light irradiation. This phenomenon suggests reinforced transformation of Fe3+ to Fe2+ under visible light irradiation, which is resulted from the participation of photogenerated electron from p-GCN. So, CDs provide high-speed interfacial electron transmission bridges between p-GCN and α-FeOOH, which significantly prevents the photogenerated electron-hole recombination and accelerates the Fe3+/Fe2+ redox cycle, leading to superior photo-Fenton catalytic performance.

Radical trapping experiments were monitored to determine the dominant reactive species in the photo-Fenton system by employing EDTA-2Na, TBA, and AA as photogenerated holes (h+), HO•, and •O2 − scavengers, respectively. Fig. 8d displays the influence of different scavengers on the degradation rate of RhB within 20 min. Obvious inhibition performance of the RhB degradation rate can be seen with the addition of scavengers, which follows the order of TBA > AA > EDTA-2Na. It is reasonable to conclude that HO•, •O2 − and h+ are generated in the photo-Fenton system to serve as reactive species for pollutant elimination. The effect of these reactive species follows the order of HO• > •O2 − > h+, implying that the HO• and •O2 − radicals are the predominant contributors. In-situ ESR analysis was employed to further demonstrate the generation of HO• and •O2 − with DMPO as the spin-trapping agent. As shown in Fig. 8e–f, no signal peaks can be observed under dark condition. Whereas characteristic strong signals of DMPO-HO• and DMPO-•O2 − appear under visible light irradiation for 5 min, with stronger signals when extended to 10 min, indicating the continuous production of HO• and •O2 − [54]. Combining the trapping experiment and ESR results, HO• and •O2 − radicals are determined as the essential active species participated in photo-Fenton process.

To elucidate the significant role of CDs for the generation reactive radicals, the concentration of HO• in α-FeOOH/p-GCN + H2O2 and α-FeOOH/CDs/p-GCN + H2O2 systems under visible light irradiation were monitored by a coumarin-based fluorescent probe method (detail in Supporting information). Excited by a wavelength of 340 nm, the characteristic fluorescence peak of formed 7-hydroxycoumarin (7OHC) at around 453 nm was detected, as displayed in Figure S9 [55]. The fluorescence intensity increases with prolonged irradiation time, indicating the continuous formation of HO• both in the α-FeOOH/p-GCN + H2O2 and α-FeOOH/CDs/p-GCN + H2O2 systems. The obvious higher fluorescence intensity at the same reaction time suggests that the impregnation of CDs strengths the generating rate of HO• in the α-FeOOH/CDs/p-GCN + H2O2 system. This result further reveals CDs provides high-speed electron transmission bridges between p-GCN and α-FeOOH to improve the conversion kinetics of Fe3+ to Fe2+, leading to efficient generation of reactive HO• radicals.

Based on the above results and analysis, the possible reaction mechanism for the photo-Fenton reaction over α-FeOOH/CDs/p-GCN was proposed and described in Scheme 2 . The reaction process is mainly composed of three parts, including the photo-excited reaction, the transport of photogenerated electrons, and the Fenton-like reaction. Firstly, the α-FeOOH/CDs/p-GCN can be excited via visible light irradiation to produce photogenerated electron-hole pairs. The photogenerated h+ in the VB can directly oxidize the organic pollutants, while the higher oxidation potential than E0(H2O/O2) (1.23 V vs. NHE) suggests partial holes can also oxidize water to produce O2 in the reaction system [56–58]. Whereas the photogenerated electrons of p-GCN (ECB = −0.71 V vs. NHE) can activate H2O2 to produce the highly active HO• species (E0(H2O2/HO•) = 0.38 V vs. NHE) [59] and react with O2 to produce reactive •O2 − radicals (E0 (O2/•O2 −) = −0.046 V vs. NHE) [60]. With the assistance of interfacial CDs bridges (the Fermi level of 0.698 V vs. NHE.), the photogenerated electrons can also be rapidly transfer from CB of p-GCN to the surface of α-FeOOH nanorods [61], which efficiently suppresses the photogenerated electron-hole recombination for the photocatalytic process. For the Fenton-like reaction, besides the sluggish kinetics of Fe3+ to Fe2+ reduced by H2O2, the transferred electrons through CDs with 0.698 V vs NHE Fermi level would readily accelerate the transformation of Fe3+ to Fe2+ (E0 (Fe3+/Fe2+) = 0.77 V vs NHE) [62], which immediately activates H2O2 to produce HO• radicals and is simultaneously oxidized to Fe3+ again. Since the reduction of Fe3+ to Fe2+ is the rate determining step for Fenton-like process, the smooth Fe3+/Fe2+ redox cycle can significantly increase the Fenton-like catalytic activity, as well as the utilization efficiency of H2O2. Through rational design of the heterostructure of α-FeOOH/CDs/p-GCN, the synergetic photo-Fenton system guarantees the efficient cooperation of photocatalysis and Fenton-like catalysis, leading to superior catalytic efficiency towards a series of recalcitrant pollutants.

In conclusion, we have successfully fabricated a ternary α-FeOOH/CDs/p-GCN composite as visible-light-driven heterogeneous photo-Fenton catalyst for contaminated water remediation. The porous structure of g-C3N4 efficiently facilitates visible light harvesting and photoexcitation efficiency. With an optimal incorporation proportion of 2 wt%, CDs are verified to be high-speed bridges between p-GCN and α-FeOOH for the transmission of photogenerated electrons, which simultaneously inhibits the recombination of electron-hole pairs and accelerates the transformation kinetics of Fe3+/Fe2+ redox cycle. The distinctive heterostructure and synergic catalytic effect endow α-FeOOH/CDs/p-GCN superior catalytic activity, excellent stability, and general usability for recalcitrant pollutant elimination. The FeOOH/CDs/p-GCN also demonstrates excellent tolerance to realistic environmental matrices including deionized water, sea water, river water, wastewater, and underground water. The catalytic mechanism of the photo-Fenton reaction over α-FeOOH/CDs/p-GCN is also proposed, and HO• and •O2 − radicals are proved to be the predominant contributors. This work provides a highly efficient photo-Fenton system for contaminated water remediation, which display the great potential for practical applications.
